items.q: What does 'piano' (p) mean as a dynamic marking?
items.why: Students see 'piano' and think of the instrument. As dynamic, p = soft (from Italian). The instrument is actually a shortened 'pianoforte' (soft-loud) because it could play at varying dynamics, unlike the harpsichord.

items.q: What distinguishes Classical (1750-1820) from Baroque style?
items.correct: Clearer melodic phrases, homophonic texture, balanced structures like sonata form.
items.random_wrong: Classical means any old serious music.
items.why: Students use 'classical' loosely. The Classical period specifically reacted against Baroque complexity - simpler textures, clearer cadences, and balanced phrase structures (Mozart, Haydn, early Beethoven).

items.q: Which orchestral family does the saxophone belong to?
items.misconception: Brass (because it is made of brass).
items.why: Students classify by material. Instrument families are defined by SOUND PRODUCTION: woodwinds use a reed or air across an edge. The sax uses a reed, so despite the brass body, it's woodwind.

items.q: Whose 1725 Gradus ad Parnassum codified species counterpoint?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Fux. “Rameau” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which famous four-note motif begins Beethoven's Fifth?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Short-short-short-long. “Long-short-short” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: In what year did Bach compose the Goldberg Variations?
items.why: Exact answer: 1741 AD. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
